text:
She climbed on. Someone clanged a bell and the whole ship broke into a scramble, as they had expected. She preferred quiet raids, but on a ship this size that was impossible.

She was among the first over the rail and crawled as fast as she could toward the nearest sailor. With a sharp twist she swept his legs with the muscle of her tail, drove her claw into his belly, and shoved him back to the others. Another splash. Someone quick had already dragged prey into the water. Two. She crawled on. Humans always forgot how fast they were even on dry wood, not knowing how briefly they could sustain it. They had to be quick or they lost the edge.

A big man stepped from the door that led down into the hold. His gun went off, and Melusine could not tell if that was why one of her sisters screamed in the din or only coincidence. They would count losses when it was over. She lunged to stop him firing again. She raked his bearded face with her claw and tried to ignore the pain that stabbed between her ribs; when her momentum died he smashed the butt of the gun into her cheek. Not enough to stop her. She had taken worse. She dragged him to the deck, coiled her tail around him, and bit into his neck. She wanted to hurt him, but if she struck an artery and he died before she could carry him down into the deeps, it would be a waste, and Melusine hated wasting prey more than anything. So she shifted her bite a few finger-widths aside and went for his temple with her blows instead, but he was a stubborn breed. Enduring kicks and punches, Mel hauled her prey toward the rail. Two sisters rushed to help, and then the man could not keep up with them. His gun barked twice more. It did him little good. Melusine locked a grip on the rail and, her tail still cinched tight around him, hurled herself into the sea.

She saw many of her sisters below, and the bodies sinking around them. They would count later. The man still fought, still thrashed, despite the blood he must have lost. He was full of life. Melusine leaned close and took his face between her hands. She looked into his brown eyes, as she looked into the eyes of all her victims. The least respect she could give. Then, swift, she sealed her mouth to his. He went rigid with shock; it had to be that, because the venom that would paralyze him could not yet have worked. Her tongue found its way between his lips. She kissed him long, deep, almost with passion. Then she let him go. His gaze glazed over and he began to sink toward the seafloor, where the others were waiting: the rest of the crew who had met the same fate, and the seafolk who would take them away.

Mel turned back toward the ship to climb for her next victim, but the young Merfolk she had sent to the bilge swam up to her. She sent Melusine a single thought: success. The skin was gone from the girl’s hands, her own blood seeping from more than one place, but the pain drowned beneath the intoxication of breaking through the planks and holing the hull. Melusine stretched her mind to her sisters again and ordered them back to the water. The humans might even have breathed easier when the fish-tailed, woman-bodied monsters leaped from the deck and the waves seemed to swallow them, but that was only wishful thinking.

The Shoal swam to the breach to widen it. When it was clear the humans had no chance of mending the hole in time, most of them crossed to the port side. At a single sign from Melusine her sisters leapt together and grabbed as high as they could to capsize the ship. A few sailors still tried to fight. All they earned was to be hurled overboard by stronger Merfolk arms ahead of their mates, who would go down with the ship. The wood groaned as the hull rocked, trying to hold itself together, but the Shoal together were stronger. Planks split and shrieked, nails sprang from their beds. When a mast swung within reach, five Merfolk seized it to drag it toward the black water, while others yanked the sailors slipping into the sea down into the depths. At last the ship began to sink. As more and more salt water poured into her belly she could not stay afloat, and everything aboard went to a watery grave.

The Merfolk rounded up the sailors, anyone still living. They were the most important now. Others would gather the useful things from the wrecks; that was not their task. Melusine herself took hold of three paralyzed sailors, and when she saw everyone waiting only for the word to go, she gave the order. “Let’s go home.”

As they swam deeper and deeper, the light died first. Melusine had never understood how they knew with such certainty that they were headed the right way, yet they never once lost their course. Perhaps they felt the voices of the Ancient Gods before they even understood it; their call pointed toward home like a compass. The earthbound, and those who liked to forget the unfathomable forces that shaped the world, not that those forces let them forget for long, often compared this sense to the whales that traveled the same paths all year with no guide at all. They simply knew the way, as their mothers had, and their mothers before them.

The road up to the surface, to the ships’ lanes, was long. The builders of their cities had not planned for where, a thousand years and a thousand more from then, humans would try to cross the ocean. Even so, the Merfolk’s tirelessness was the legacy of their human forebears. They could swim for days if a goal drove them.

As they dropped lower, closer to home, Mel began to see again. There were thin threads of light here their refined eyes could catch. The silhouette of a vast underwater mountain range took shape ahead. When she looked down she saw the crowns of ruined towers jutting from the seabed. A great metropolis had stood here once, a home to more than a million Sirens, an ancient, noble people in a dark age when the Gods of the Depths still ruled the ocean. Now the ruins stood only as waymarks pointing toward Geynea, one of the Merfolk’s Five Cities, where Melusine and her Shoal lived. They had never been so many that rebuilding it all would have been worth it.

The water began to warm. She never truly froze, not even in the coldest water, but she felt the change, and warmth had always sent a pleasant shiver through her. Like pale veins in a giant body, the lava flows of the volcanic range traced themselves ahead, fire under water that radiated heat and fed Geynea with light and power. Its vast dome slowly took shape in the distance.
